---
title: "The Microsoft Identity and Governance Stack"
subtitle: "Native Capabilities and Inherited Limitations"
doc-type: orgpath-narrative-chapter
audience: [Technical, Customer]
classification: Controlled
boundary: GCC-Moderate
family: orgpath-narrative
series-order: 01
status: Published
canon-source: "src/uiao/canon/UIAO_009_Microsoft_Coverage_And_Gap_Doctrine_v1.0.md"
source-document: "The Microsoft Identity and Governance Stack — Native Capabilities and Inherited Limitations.md"
rendered-from: "Pandoc-converted Markdown sidecar of OneDrive .docx; body preserved unchanged."
---

## Chapter 1 — The Governance Gap at the Center of the Microsoft Cloud

### What Active Directory Was, and Why It Mattered

For more than two decades, Active Directory Domain Services served as the single unifying substrate of enterprise and federal IT infrastructure. Its significance was never merely that it authenticated users or applied policy to machines. Its significance was architectural: Active Directory was the one system in which every object, every identity, every machine, every policy, and every administrative delegation lived together, related to one another through a coherent structural model, and expressed the organization's governance posture as a matter of native function. The Organizational Unit was not merely a container for grouping users. It was a positional statement. It told the directory where a user sat in the institution's hierarchy, and that position carried weight — weight expressed through the automatic inheritance of Group Policy, through the scope of delegated administrative authority, through the application of fine-grained security settings, and through the replication of DNS records tied to the domain's infrastructure. A user in the Finance OU of the Washington Division received, automatically and deterministically, a specific set of policies because of where they sat, not because an administrator had enumerated their name against every policy. That is the feature that is missing from the cloud.

The components of that unified substrate were numerous and tightly coupled. The forest and domain provided the outermost administrative boundary and the trust model. The OU tree provided the internal structure — a named, hierarchical arrangement of containers into which every managed identity and every managed machine was placed. GPOs were linked to nodes in that tree and inherited downward through it, allowing administrators to define policy at the domain level, override it at the division level, and specialize it further at the unit level, all without enumerating individual objects. The enterprise Kerberos Key Distribution Center was inseparable from the domain controller. The service locator DNS records that every Kerberos client depended on were published automatically into the AD-integrated DNS zone, which itself replicated with the directory. The enterprise Certificate Authority validated enrollment identity against the directory, issued certificates through auto-enrollment driven by Group Policy, and embedded CRL distribution points whose addresses were themselves resolvable through the AD-integrated DNS. LDAP provided a single query interface that allowed any tool — any auditing system, any management console, any reporting engine — to traverse the entire object graph and understand the organizational context of any identity it encountered. These were not features that coexisted in a suite. They were expressions of a single unified data model, administered through a single inheritance framework, enforcing a single governance posture.

### How Cloud Migration Breaks the Unification

The migration from Active Directory to the Microsoft cloud stack is not, as it is sometimes characterized, a straightforward lift-and-shift of function. It is a decomposition. The individual capabilities that existed as integrated facets of Active Directory are separated into distinct products: Entra ID for identity, Intune for device management, Azure Arc for server governance, Azure DNS for name resolution, Microsoft Cloud PKI for certificate issuance, and a constellation of Defender products for security visibility. Each of these products is genuinely capable within its own domain. Each is the product of substantial engineering investment. Each represents a significant advancement over the on-premises equivalent in specific dimensions — scalability, global availability, integration with modern authentication protocols, and resistance to certain categories of perimeter-based attack. But each of these tools has its own governance model, its own scoping mechanism, its own data model, and its own understanding of what the organization looks like. And critically, none of them shares a common substrate with any of the others.

Entra ID does not know what Intune knows about a device's organizational assignment. Intune does not know what Azure Arc knows about a server's on-premises heritage. Azure Policy does not know what Entra ID Governance knows about a user's lifecycle state. Azure DNS does not know what AD-integrated DNS knew about the organizational context of the machines that registered records in it. Microsoft Cloud PKI does not know what AD CS knew about the certificate templates that were appropriate for a given organizational unit's machines. The Defender products each see a slice of the environment and generate intelligence within that slice, but the correlation of that intelligence across slices requires custom integration work that does not exist natively in any of the products. This is the architecture of the Microsoft cloud identity and governance stack as it actually exists — not as a unified platform, but as a collection of individually capable point tools that do not inherit from one another and do not share a structural model of the organization they govern.

[DIAGRAM-01: Side-by-side comparison diagram. Left panel labeled "On-prem: one unified substrate" centers on a single Active Directory box (OU tree + GPO + Kerberos + AD-integrated DNS + ADCS + LDAP) with four downward arrows to outputs: Organizational position (DN expresses lineage), Policy inheritance (GPO flows down OU tree), Delegated authority (ACL on container, inherited), Dependency graph (LDAP traversal). Right panel labeled "Cloud: decomposed point tools, no shared substrate" shows six disconnected boxes with no connecting lines between them: Entra ID (flat directory), Intune (group-based targeting), Azure Arc (ARM control plane), Azure DNS, Microsoft Cloud PKI, Defender suite (siloed signals). Six dashed arrows fan out from the left-panel AD box to each of the six cloud boxes, labeled "cloud migration decomposes into" on the topmost arrow only. Engineering blueprint style, neutral slate background with federal navy (#1F3A5F) for AD and teal (#1A9E8F) for cloud boxes, no human figures, 16:9 landscape.]

### The Governance Questions That Cannot Be Answered Without Custom Engineering

Active Directory could answer a specific set of governance questions as a matter of native function. Where does this identity sit in the organization? The LDAP distinguished name answered that question with precision: it expressed the complete path from the object to the domain root through every OU in the hierarchy, and any system that could issue an LDAP query could retrieve that path without additional configuration. What policies does that organizational position imply? The Group Policy resultant set calculation answered that question deterministically: given the object's position in the OU tree and the GPOs linked at each level of that tree, the effective policy was computable by any tool with read access to the domain. Who has delegated authority over this segment of the organization? The ACL on the OU answered that question: the Access Control Entries on the container object expressed precisely which security principals had which permissions over objects within that container, inheriting downward unless explicitly blocked. What is the blast radius of retiring this dependency? The dependency graph of objects — users, computers, groups, certificates, GPOs, trusts — could be traversed through LDAP to identify every consumer of every resource before any change was made.

None of these questions can be answered by the Microsoft cloud stack without custom engineering. There is no cloud object whose attributes express the complete organizational path of an identity. There is no cloud policy engine that can compute effective policy from the intersection of an identity's organizational position and a hierarchy of policy objects linked to that position. There is no cloud delegation model that expresses authority as a right over a subtree rather than as a right over an explicitly enumerated set of objects. There is no cloud tool that traverses the combined dependency graph of Entra ID objects, Intune policies, Azure resources, DNS records, and certificates before a governance change is made. These are not features that are planned for a future product release. They are structural absences that flow from the architectural decision to build a cloud platform as a collection of specialized services rather than as a unified directory.

### Why This Is an Architectural Gap, Not an Operational One

It is important to be precise about the nature of this limitation, because it is frequently mischaracterized. Organizations that encounter it in practice sometimes describe it as a documentation problem: the organization has not adequately mapped its on-premises structure before beginning migration. Others describe it as a training problem: administrators who understand the cloud tools will find ways to approximate the behavior of the on-premises substrate. Neither characterization is accurate. The gap is architectural. It is built into the design of the cloud platform. No amount of documentation can add a structural inheritance model to a flat directory that has no inheritance model. No amount of training can make a policy engine express organizational position as a targeting condition when the policy engine has no concept of organizational position. No amount of operational discipline can make five disconnected tools share a single data model that none of them was built to consume.

This distinction matters for federal agencies in particular, because the consequence of mischaracterizing the gap is that migration programs are planned against an assumed destination state that does not exist. Teams complete the technical migration — they synchronize identities, enroll devices, deploy Arc agents, configure DNS, establish Cloud PKI — and then discover, at the point of decommissioning the on-premises infrastructure, that the governance posture they believed they had reconstructed in the cloud is in fact a collection of individually functional configurations that cannot collectively express what they expressed on premises as a matter of course. The migration is complete in the technical sense. The governance coherence that motivated the original infrastructure is absent from the destination state. That absence is not a remediation item that can be addressed after the fact with additional configuration. It requires either an acceptance of a reduced governance posture or the construction of a custom substrate that the platform does not natively provide.

## Chapter 2 — Active Directory Domain Services — The Unified Substrate

### Architecture and Native Capabilities

Active Directory Domain Services is a directory service based on the Lightweight Directory Access Protocol and the X.500 directory information model, introduced by Microsoft with Windows 2000 Server and continuously extended through successive Windows Server releases. Its foundational architectural unit is the domain: a replication boundary, an authentication boundary, and an administrative boundary that simultaneously. A forest is a collection of domains that share a common schema, a common configuration partition, and a transitive trust relationship with one another. Every domain in a forest shares the Global Catalog, which holds a partial replica of every object in every domain and allows forest-wide searches without traversing inter-domain referrals. The forest root domain holds the Schema Master and Domain Naming Master FSMO roles, which govern schema modification and domain creation respectively, establishing a clear hierarchy of control over the directory's fundamental capabilities.

Within a domain, the Organizational Unit is the primary structural element. The OU is a container object derived from the X.500 organizationalUnit class, and it exists for two distinct purposes: to organize objects visually and conceptually, and to serve as the attachment point for governance mechanisms. These two purposes are inseparable in the AD model. Group Policy Objects are linked to OUs. Administrative delegation is expressed by setting Access Control Entries on OU objects. Fine-grained password policies in functional level 2008 and above are applied through Password Settings Objects linked to global security groups, but the OU remains the primary policy inheritance vehicle for the overwhelming majority of enterprise configuration. The inheritance model is hierarchical and deterministic: a GPO linked to a parent OU applies to all objects in all child OUs unless explicitly blocked by the No Override or Block Inheritance flags. This inheritance flows without any additional configuration from the administrator. Placing an object in an OU is the act that determines which policies apply to it.

### Authentication, DNS Integration, and the Service Locator Model

Authentication in Active Directory is provided primarily by Kerberos version 5, with NTLM available as a fallback for legacy systems. The Kerberos KDC runs on every domain controller. When a client authenticates, it contacts the KDC to obtain a Ticket-Granting Ticket, which it then presents to obtain service tickets for specific resources. The KDC is located not through a static configuration but through a dynamic DNS query: the client queries the DNS zone for SRV records of the form \_kerberos.\_tcp.dc.\_msdcs.domain.com, and the DNS server returns the addresses of available domain controllers. This service locator model means that AD's authentication infrastructure is inseparable from its DNS infrastructure. DNS is not a convenience feature layered on top of AD; it is a required component of every authentication event. AD-integrated DNS zones are stored in the domain partition or in dedicated application partitions within the directory itself, and zone data replicates automatically with the directory replication topology. Dynamic DNS registration allows domain-joined machines to register and update their A and PTR records without administrator intervention, keeping the DNS zone current as machines move and addresses change.

The LDAP interface provides a uniform query model for the entire directory. Any object in the directory — user, computer, group, OU, GPO link, certificate template, service connection point, schema class definition — is reachable through a standard LDAP query. The distinguished name of any object expresses its complete position in the directory hierarchy, from the object itself through every container and OU to the domain root. Tools that need to understand the organizational context of an object query its DN and parse the OU path. Tools that need to find all objects in a specific organizational unit issue an LDAP search scoped to that OU with appropriate search scope. This uniformity is not incidental — it is the feature that made Active Directory the governance substrate for an entire generation of enterprise tooling. Monitoring systems, security tools, provisioning engines, reporting platforms, and compliance scanners all built against LDAP because LDAP provided a complete and consistent picture of the organization's identity and structural landscape.

### Replication, FSMO Roles, and Schema

Active Directory replication uses a multi-master model in which every domain controller can accept writes and replicate changes to other domain controllers through a replication topology managed by the Knowledge Consistency Checker. Replication is governed by site topology, with site links defining the cost and schedule of inter-site replication. Within a site, replication is near-real-time; across sites, it follows the configured schedule. Five Flexible Single Master Operations roles create exceptions to the multi-master model: the PDC Emulator handles password changes and time synchronization; the RID Master allocates pools of Relative Identifiers to domain controllers for object creation; the Infrastructure Master tracks cross-domain group-to-user references; the Schema Master controls schema modifications; and the Domain Naming Master controls domain creation and removal. These role holders are dependencies in the domain topology, and their loss creates operational degradation that ranges from inconvenient to catastrophic depending on the role and the duration of the outage.

The schema defines every object class and every attribute in the directory. Schema extensions are permanent: once an attribute or object class is added to the schema, it cannot be removed without restoring from backup, and even then its unique OID remains registered. This irreversibility is a significant operational constraint. Organizations that extended the AD schema for third-party products — Lync, Exchange, SCCM, custom applications — carry those extensions indefinitely, and those extensions appear in the schema partition of every domain controller in the forest. The schema master must be available for any schema modification, and schema changes replicate to every domain controller in the forest before taking effect on most objects.

### Limitations of Active Directory in the Contemporary Environment

The limitations of Active Directory in the context of contemporary federal IT are substantial and well understood. AD is an on-premises system that requires domain connectivity to function as designed. Machines that are not domain-joined receive no GPO. Users who are not connected to the corporate network cannot authenticate through Kerberos. Branch offices with inadequate connectivity experience replication lag and authentication failures when the WAN link to the nearest domain controller is degraded. The perimeter-based network model that AD was designed for — a trusted interior protected by a firewall, with all managed endpoints inside that perimeter — no longer accurately describes the environment in which federal agencies operate. Mobile devices, remote workers, cloud-hosted workloads, and partner interconnections have eroded the perimeter, and AD's security model has not adapted to this erosion.

The privileged access model of Active Directory is catastrophically tiered. Domain Admins and Enterprise Admins have unrestricted access to every object in the domain or forest respectively. There is no native mechanism to constrain Domain Admin access to a subset of the directory — the membership of that group is either present or absent, and presence confers full control. This creates an attack surface that is correspondingly wide: the compromise of a single Domain Admin credential, by any means, gives an attacker complete control over every identity, every machine, every policy, and every secret in the domain. The most consequential attack techniques targeting AD — Golden Ticket attacks that forge Kerberos tickets using the krbtgt account hash, DCSync attacks that replicate all domain secrets from a compromised account, Pass-the-Hash attacks that reuse NTLM credential material without requiring plaintext passwords, and Kerberoasting attacks that extract service account credentials from Kerberos service tickets — all exploit structural features of the Kerberos and NTLM protocols as implemented in AD, and none of them can be fully mitigated without replacing the underlying authentication architecture. AD is not merely aging; it is a perennial target for sophisticated adversaries who have invested years in developing techniques that exploit its structural properties.

The GPO model, for all its power, is genuinely opaque. The effective configuration of any given machine is the product of all GPOs that apply to it through site, domain, and OU linkage, filtered by WMI conditions and security group membership, resolved through the precedence rules of the resultant set of policy calculation, and applied by client-side extensions that interpret binary ADMX-encoded settings. No single tool presents the complete effective configuration of a machine in a human-readable, auditable form without running gpresult or RSOP on the machine itself. GPOs are not source-controlled by default. There is no native change history beyond the timestamp on the GPO container object in the directory. The state of any machine's actual configuration — whether it received the policy that was intended, whether that policy is still current, whether a GPO was changed after last application — is not knowable without querying the machine directly. This opacity is a significant limitation in environments that require continuous configuration assurance.

## Chapter 3 — Microsoft Entra ID — The Flat Directory

### Architecture and Native Capabilities

Microsoft Entra ID, formerly Azure Active Directory, is Microsoft's cloud-native identity platform. It serves as the identity and access management foundation for Microsoft 365, Azure, and any third-party application that supports modern authentication protocols. Entra ID provides authentication through OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect, and SAML 2.0, covering both browser-based and API-based access patterns. Its object model includes users, groups, service principals, application registrations, managed identities, and device objects. Users can be cloud-native — created directly in the tenant — or synchronized from on-premises Active Directory. Groups can be of three types: security groups for access control and policy targeting, Microsoft 365 groups for collaboration with associated SharePoint, Teams, and Exchange resources, and distribution groups for email distribution. Application registrations represent applications that use the Entra ID identity platform, while service principals are the per-tenant instance of an application registration, carrying the actual permission grants and role assignments for a specific tenant. Managed identities — system-assigned or user-assigned — provide Azure workloads with an Entra ID identity that can be used to authenticate to other Azure services without requiring stored credentials.

The Entra ID role-based access control model assigns directory roles — Global Administrator, User Administrator, Authentication Administrator, Security Reader, and dozens of others — to users, groups, and service principals. Role assignments can be direct and permanent, or they can be made eligible through Privileged Identity Management, requiring just-in-time activation. The unified audit log captures all sign-in events and directory change events, retaining them for between 30 and 90 days depending on license tier. The Microsoft Graph API provides comprehensive programmatic access to every object in the directory through a RESTful interface that supports filtering, paging, and delta queries for efficient synchronization. The My Apps portal provides users with a consolidated view of all applications assigned to them, supporting SAML and OpenID Connect single sign-on. The B2B collaboration model allows users from external tenants to be invited as guest users, receiving a cross-tenant identity that allows them to access shared resources without requiring full directory membership.

### Directory Extensions and the Attribute Model

Entra ID supports the extension of the user, group, device, and application object schemas through directory extension attributes. These extensions are registered to an application registration and expressed as additional properties on the objects they extend. They allow organizations to store custom attributes — employee classification codes, clearance levels, organizational codes, cost center identifiers — directly on directory objects, where they become queryable through Graph API and usable in dynamic group membership rules. Extension attributes are not schema-global in the AD sense; they are tied to the application registration that defines them and must be declared before they can be populated. The total number of extension attributes is bounded, and the extension model does not support complex types or structured data — extension values are string, integer, boolean, or binary scalars. This model is sufficient for storing simple organizational metadata but is not designed to express organizational hierarchy or structural relationships between objects.

### What Entra ID Does Not Natively Provide

The most consequential architectural fact about Entra ID, from the perspective of organizations migrating from Active Directory, is that it has no organizational hierarchy. The directory is flat. All users, all groups, all devices, all service principals exist at the same level within the tenant. There is no container object analogous to the Organizational Unit. There is no parent-child relationship between user objects and any structural container that would cause policies to flow from the container to the objects it holds. This is not a limitation of the current release that is anticipated to be addressed in a future version. It is a foundational architectural choice. Entra ID was designed as a multi-tenant, globally distributed identity service optimized for authentication and application access. It was not designed as a directory information tree in the X.500 tradition. Those are different things, and Entra ID is the former.

The practical consequence is direct and far-reaching. Administrative delegation in Active Directory can be expressed as a right over a subtree: a helpdesk administrator delegated to the Washington Division OU can manage every user and computer in that OU and every child OU beneath it, without any enumeration of those objects. In Entra ID, there is no subtree to delegate over. Every delegation is either tenant-wide — the administrator role applies to every object of the applicable type in the tenant — or scoped to an Administrative Unit, which is a flat container with no inheritance property, discussed separately in Chapter 6. A user's organizational position is not a native attribute of the Entra ID user object. There is no directional parent relationship from a user to the division they belong to. There is no mechanism by which the assignment of a user to a division automatically changes any policy that applies to them. Every policy assignment — Conditional Access, Intune configuration profile, application access, Entra ID Governance access package — is constructed through explicit group membership or explicit object selection, not through positional inference. The tenant boundary is the only boundary Entra ID natively recognizes. Everything inside it is flat, and everything is governed by explicit assignment rather than structural inheritance.

## Chapter 4 — Entra Connect and Cloud Sync — Synchronization Without Structure

### Architecture and Native Capabilities

Microsoft Entra Connect is the primary synchronization engine that copies identity objects from on-premises Active Directory to Entra ID. It operates through a connector-based architecture: the Active Directory Connector reads objects from one or more on-premises AD forests, transforms them through the Metadirectory using synchronization rules, and the Entra ID Connector writes the result to the cloud tenant. Objects synchronized include users, groups, contacts, and devices. The attribute-level filtering capability controls which attributes of each object are included in the synchronized record — organizations can exclude sensitive attributes or include custom attributes by configuring the synchronization rules engine. OU-level filtering scopes which portions of the on-premises directory are included: only objects within the selected OUs are synchronized, allowing organizations to exclude administrative OUs, service account OUs, or staging OUs from the cloud tenant.

Entra Connect supports three authentication options for synchronized users. Password hash synchronization copies a cryptographic hash of the user's password hash to Entra ID, allowing cloud authentication against the synchronized credential material without requiring network connectivity back to on-premises infrastructure. Pass-through authentication authenticates cloud sign-in attempts by forwarding them through a lightweight agent to the on-premises domain controllers, keeping credential validation on premises. Federation with Active Directory Federation Services redirects authentication to an on-premises or cloud-hosted claims provider, allowing organizations to enforce on-premises authentication policies for cloud access. Seamless single sign-on, available with both password hash sync and pass-through authentication, allows domain-joined devices to authenticate to Entra ID silently using Kerberos tickets obtained from the domain, without requiring users to re-enter credentials. Writeback features — password writeback, group writeback, device writeback — allow changes made in Entra ID to be propagated back to on-premises AD, enabling a degree of bidirectional consistency for specific attribute domains.

Microsoft Entra Cloud Sync is a lighter-weight synchronization alternative that uses a cloud-hosted provisioning engine with a minimal on-premises agent, rather than the full Entra Connect infrastructure. Cloud Sync was designed primarily to address multi-forest synchronization scenarios and to reduce the operational complexity of the on-premises synchronization infrastructure. Its provisioning scope is configured through the cloud portal rather than on-premises tooling, and it supports a subset of the transformation and filtering capabilities of the full Entra Connect engine. Cloud Sync is appropriate for organizations with simpler synchronization requirements or specific multi-forest architectures where the Entra Connect agent deployment model is operationally impractical.

### What Synchronization Carries and What It Does Not

Synchronization copies object attributes. It does not copy organizational structure. When an Entra Connect synchronization run processes a user object in the on-premises AD, it reads the configured attributes of that user — including, by default, the onPremisesDistinguishedName attribute, which carries the full LDAP path of the user in the AD directory tree — and writes those attributes to the corresponding user object in Entra ID. The distinguished name is available as a string property on the synchronized user object in Entra ID. That string carries information: it tells any reader who queries it that the user was, at the time of last synchronization, located at a specific position in the on-premises OU hierarchy. But that information is inert. No native Entra ID capability reads the onPremisesDistinguishedName and derives from it any policy application, any scope assignment, or any governance posture. The string sits in the attribute, unused by the platform itself.

This is a critical distinction that is often overlooked during migration planning. The OU path of a user is preserved in Entra ID in the sense that the string is queryable through Graph API. It is not preserved in the sense that matters for governance: no policy flows from it, no delegation is derived from it, and no capability of the Entra ID platform consumes it as structural information. The OU hierarchy exists in Active Directory. A shadow of its path expression appears as a string value in a synchronized attribute. The governance meaning of that path — the policy inheritance, the delegation scope, the organizational context — does not make the journey. It remains in the on-premises system, and when the on-premises system is decommissioned, the governance meaning disappears with it, leaving only the string.

Furthermore, synchronization is unidirectional in its authoritative source model. Synchronized objects are mastered on-premises: the on-premises AD is the source of truth for those objects, and changes to synchronized attributes made directly in Entra ID are overwritten on the next synchronization cycle. This means that organizations in the process of decommissioning Active Directory cannot fully migrate management authority to the cloud until synchronization itself is retired. The synchronization relationship must be maintained as long as on-premises AD remains the master, and on-premises AD must remain the master as long as synchronization is running. Breaking out of this dependency requires converting synchronized objects to cloud-native objects — a process that requires careful planning and that severs the link between the cloud identity and the on-premises identity, with implications for all applications and services that reference the on-premises objectSid or objectGuid.

## Chapter 5 — Group Policy Objects — The Configuration Inheritance Engine

### Architecture and Native Capabilities

Group Policy Objects are the primary mechanism by which Windows system configuration is applied to domain-joined machines and users in an Active Directory environment. A GPO is a paired data structure: a Group Policy Container, stored in the Active Directory domain partition and holding metadata such as the GPO's GUID, version number, and security descriptor; and a Group Policy Template, stored in the SYSVOL share of every domain controller and holding the actual policy settings files, ADMX-based administrative template definitions, scripts, and software installation packages. GPOs are created and managed through the Group Policy Management Console, which presents the combined view of both storage locations. The GPO itself has no effect until it is linked to a site, domain, or Organizational Unit. Linking a GPO to an OU is the act that causes the policy to apply to objects within that OU and, by inheritance, to objects within all child OUs, subject to filtering and precedence rules.

The inheritance model is the defining characteristic of the GPO system. When the Group Policy Client service applies policy to a machine or user, it retrieves the list of applicable GPOs by walking up the OU hierarchy from the object's container to the domain root, collecting all linked GPOs at each level, and building an ordered list that reflects the inheritance hierarchy. Site-linked GPOs apply first, domain-linked GPOs next, and OU-linked GPOs last, with child OU GPOs taking precedence over parent OU GPOs for settings that conflict. WMI filters condition the application of individual GPOs on the result of a WMI query against the target machine, allowing administrators to scope a GPO to machines that meet specific hardware or software conditions without managing separate OUs for each condition. Security filtering controls which security principals actually receive a given GPO by requiring that they have both Read and Apply Group Policy permissions on the GPO object; removing Apply Group Policy from a group effectively exempts that group's members from the policy regardless of their OU position.

### Policy Scope and Coverage

The breadth of GPO coverage across the Windows configuration surface is comprehensive. Computer configuration settings apply to the machine at startup regardless of which user is logged on, covering security settings (account policy, local policies, audit policy, user rights assignments, security options), software installation, scripts executed at startup and shutdown, folder redirection for computer-side paths, Windows Firewall rules, certificate distribution through autoenrollment and trusted root configuration, and the full body of Administrative Template settings that expose registry-backed configuration for the operating system and its components. User configuration settings apply when a specific user logs on, covering analogous software, script, folder redirection, and Administrative Template settings scoped to user-side registry paths and user-side Windows features. The Administrative Templates model allows any application vendor or organization to extend the policy schema by depositing ADMX and ADML files in the SYSVOL Central Store, making new settings available to all administrators through the GPMC without requiring any modification to the schema or the AD directory itself.

### Limitations of Group Policy in the Contemporary Environment

Group Policy is exclusively an on-premises, domain-joined construct. Its application mechanism requires that the machine processing policy be able to communicate with a domain controller at startup and at logon. Non-domain-joined devices receive no Group Policy. Cloud-native workloads that are not domain-connected receive no Group Policy. Managed mobile devices — iOS, Android — receive no Group Policy. A machine that is domain-joined but geographically separated from domain controllers by an unreliable WAN link will process its cached policy at startup but will fail to retrieve updated policy until connectivity is restored. The policy applied to a machine that has been off-network for an extended period may be materially stale, and there is no native mechanism that continuously verifies the currency of applied policy against the currently-linked GPOs.

The programmatic auditability of Group Policy is severely limited. The effective configuration of a machine is determined by the combined effect of all applicable GPOs, the WMI and security filter conditions that may or may not have evaluated as expected, the precedence resolution among conflicting settings, and the client-side extension processing of each policy area. No single system presents this effective configuration without querying the target machine. The gpresult command produces a report of the GPOs that applied and the settings that resulted, but it must be run on or against the target machine and reflects the state at the time of the last policy refresh cycle. GPOs are not version-controlled by any native mechanism. The Group Policy Management Console allows GPOs to be backed up and restored, but backup is a point-in-time snapshot, not a versioned history. There is no native diff between two versions of a GPO's settings, no change log beyond the modification timestamp on the GPO Container object in AD, and no preview of the combined effect of all applicable GPOs before they are applied to a machine. Group Policy is, in this precise sense, an opaque, distributed, and difficult-to-audit configuration system whose actual enforcement state is known only to the endpoints that have applied it.

## Chapter 6 — Administrative Units and Dynamic Groups — Partial Compensation for Missing Structure

### Administrative Units

Administrative Units are scoped containers in Entra ID that allow directory roles to be assigned with a restricted scope. When an administrator assigns the User Administrator role scoped to an Administrative Unit, that administrator can manage only the users who are members of that AU — they cannot view, modify, or reset credentials for users outside its membership. AUs can similarly scope the Password Administrator, Authentication Administrator, Groups Administrator, and Helpdesk Administrator roles. This provides a mechanism for delegating specific administrative functions to personnel who should not have tenant-wide access: an agency's regional helpdesk can be given password reset capability scoped to the AU representing their region's user population, without being able to affect users in other regions. AU membership can be managed manually, by direct administrative assignment of individual users, groups, or devices; or it can be managed through dynamic membership rules that evaluate user or device attributes against specified conditions, automatically adding or removing objects from the AU as their attributes change.

Administrative Units do not create inheritance between themselves. Each AU is a discrete, independent container. There is no parent-child relationship between AUs. An administrator cannot create an AU hierarchy in which roles assigned to a parent AU flow down to apply within all child AUs. Creating a multi-level organizational structure using AUs requires a separate AU for each level of the hierarchy, independent membership management for each AU, and independent role assignments at each level. There is no native model in Entra ID for expressing that a role assigned at the division level implies a subset of that authority at the unit level within it. If a user's organizational position changes — they move from one division to another — their AU membership must be updated explicitly, either through a manual reassignment or through an attribute change that triggers a dynamic membership rule evaluation. The platform does not propagate any consequence of an organizational move beyond the attribute values that are directly updated.

### Dynamic Groups

Dynamic groups in Entra ID automatically maintain their membership based on rules that evaluate user or device attributes against specified conditions. A dynamic group rule can specify, for example, that membership includes all users whose department attribute equals "Finance" and whose accountEnabled attribute is true. The Entra ID service evaluates these rules against all user or device objects in the tenant and updates group membership accordingly whenever relevant attributes change. Dynamic groups support a range of rule operators — equals, not equals, starts with, ends with, contains, not contains, match (regular expression), and others — against a defined set of queryable user and device attributes. They can be used as targets for application assignment, license assignment, Conditional Access policy scoping, Intune policy targeting, and Entra ID Governance access package assignment, making them a central mechanism for policy application in the flat Entra ID directory.

Dynamic group membership processing is asynchronous. When an attribute change occurs on a user object — for example, when a user is moved between departments and their department attribute is updated — the dynamic group service must evaluate all groups whose rules reference that attribute, determine which groups the user should now be a member of and which they should be removed from, and update the membership records accordingly. This processing does not occur instantaneously. In large tenants with complex rule sets, membership updates can lag behind attribute changes by minutes to hours. During this lag period, the user's actual group membership does not reflect their current attributes, and any policy, application assignment, or license grant that depends on that group membership is not yet accurate. The platform does not provide a native alert when dynamic group membership is known to be stale or when a membership update is pending. There is no platform-guaranteed maximum latency for dynamic membership updates. The lag is acknowledged in Microsoft's documentation as a platform behavior, but it is not bounded in a way that allows organizations to make definitive statements about the currency of their policy application at any given moment.

Dynamic groups cannot reference other dynamic groups in their membership rules. A rule that attempts to express "members of Group A whose attribute also satisfies condition B" cannot be constructed: dynamic group rules evaluate directory attributes directly, not group memberships. Nested dynamic logic requires creating separate groups with separate rules and cannot be expressed as a dependency chain. This means that organizational hierarchies expressed through dynamic groups must be reconstructed at each level with independent rules, and any change to the organizational taxonomy requires a review and update of every affected group's rule, with no native tooling to identify which rules must change as a result of a structural reorganization.

## Chapter 7 — Entra ID Governance — Lifecycle Management at the Identity Level

### Architecture and Native Capabilities

Entra ID Governance is a licensed capability set that extends the core Entra ID platform with tools for managing the lifecycle of identities and their access to resources. It encompasses four primary capabilities. Access Reviews provide a periodic, structured prompt to designated reviewers to attest that specific users should retain their current access to specific resources or roles. Reviews can be configured for application access, group membership, directory role membership, or privileged role eligibility. When a review expires, the platform can take automatic action on items that were not reviewed — typically removing access — reducing the operational burden of maintaining the least-privilege principle over time. Entitlement Management provides a catalog and access package model: administrators define catalogs of resources and bundle them into access packages that represent the set of access rights needed for a specific role or function. Users or groups can request access packages through a self-service portal, triggering an approval workflow that routes the request to one or more designated approvers before granting the bundled access. Lifecycle Workflows automate identity provisioning and deprovisioning tasks triggered by HR-driven lifecycle events — onboarding new employees, managing internal transfers, and offboarding departing employees — through a set of configurable tasks executed by the platform when the triggering condition is met. Privileged Identity Management provides just-in-time activation for Entra ID directory roles and Azure RBAC roles, allowing administrators to be eligible for a privileged role without holding it permanently, requiring an explicit activation step with justification, optional approval, and a time-bounded assignment window.

### Limitations of Entra ID Governance

Entra ID Governance operates at the identity and role assignment level, not at the organizational structure level. Access Reviews evaluate whether a specific user should retain a specific assignment — a group membership, a role, an application access grant. They do not evaluate whether that assignment is appropriate given the user's organizational position, because the governance system has no authoritative record of organizational position. A reviewer conducting an Access Review of the membership of the Global Administrator role is presented with the list of members and asked to confirm or deny each one. The review UI provides the user's display name, principal name, and the date of the last sign-in. It does not provide the user's organizational placement, their reporting chain, their peer group, or any contextual information that would allow the reviewer to judge whether the assignment is structurally appropriate. That contextual information does not exist as structured data in the directory.

Entitlement Management's access package model requires administrators to define the appropriate bundles of access for each role or organizational position, and those definitions must be created and maintained manually. There is no mechanism by which the system derives what access package is appropriate for a user based on their organizational placement — the organizational placement is not modeled, and the inference from position to appropriate access is not a capability the platform provides. Lifecycle Workflows address the joiner-mover-leaver lifecycle by triggering on attribute values provided by the HR system, but the mapping between HR organizational data and the Entra ID attribute model is a custom integration requirement. HR systems express organizational structure in their own data models — cost center codes, position identifiers, supervisory relationships — and the translation of those models into Entra ID attributes that Lifecycle Workflows can consume requires an integration layer that each organization must build and maintain independently. PIM manages role activation but does not evaluate the appropriateness of roles relative to the user's organizational context. A PIM-eligible role assignment that is entirely inconsistent with a user's actual function will be activated on request as long as the configured approval workflow is satisfied, because the workflow has no access to organizational context and the platform provides no mechanism to supply it.

## Chapter 8 — Entra ID Protection — Risk-Based Signal Without Organizational Context

### Architecture and Native Capabilities

Entra ID Protection is a risk detection and remediation capability that analyzes authentication and directory events to produce user risk scores and sign-in risk scores. User risk reflects the probability that a given identity has been compromised, based on signals collected over time. Sign-in risk reflects the probability that a specific authentication attempt is not being made by the identity's legitimate owner, based on signals available at authentication time. The risk scoring engine draws on Microsoft's global threat intelligence network, which processes billions of authentications per day across Microsoft's cloud services and applies machine learning models trained on known attack patterns and legitimate usage behaviors. Signals that contribute to risk scores include leaked credential detection from public and dark-web credential dumps, atypical travel indicating authentication from geographically distant locations within a timeframe that is not physically plausible, anonymous IP address usage from known VPN or proxy infrastructure, malware-linked IP addresses associated with known botnet command-and-control infrastructure, unfamiliar sign-in properties reflecting a change in the device, location, or ASN from which a user typically authenticates, and token anomaly signals indicating the reuse of authentication tokens across contexts where they should not appear.

Risk signals feed into Conditional Access through the user risk and sign-in risk condition types, allowing organizations to require step-up MFA for medium-risk sign-ins, block high-risk sign-ins pending remediation, or require password change for users with elevated user risk. The Risky Users and Risky Sign-ins reports provide administrators with an operational dashboard of current risk state, surfacing individual risk detections with their classification, severity, and the specific signals that triggered them. Administrators can manually confirm compromise — marking a detection as accurate and escalating the user to a high-risk state — or dismiss individual detections that are known to be false positives, with the dismissal feeding back into the risk model.

### Limitations of Risk-Based Scoring Without Organizational Context

Risk scoring in Entra ID Protection is calibrated against Microsoft's global baseline of user authentication behavior, not against the behavioral norms of any specific organization's population or any specific role within that population. A user in a high-tempo operational role who routinely authenticates from multiple locations, uses VPN infrastructure, operates across unusual hours due to shift schedules, or moves between facilities in a manner that produces apparent geolocation anomalies may be persistently flagged at elevated risk levels — not because their behavior indicates compromise, but because their behavior is statistically unusual relative to the global average of Microsoft tenant users. The converse is equally significant: a sophisticated adversary conducting a patient, slow-moving intrusion that mimics the user's established behavioral baseline — authenticating from the user's typical location, at typical hours, using typical device characteristics — may produce no meaningful risk signal whatsoever, because the attack is indistinguishable from normal behavior at the individual signal level.

Risk signals are aggregated at the tenant level. The platform has no model of organizational segments within a tenant. It cannot apply different baseline models to different populations — applying a higher tolerance for location variance to field personnel and a lower tolerance to administrative roles — because the population segments do not exist as addressable entities in the risk model. Conditional Access can apply different policies to different groups based on their risk level, but the risk level assigned to each user is computed against the same global baseline regardless of their organizational function. The resulting risk signal is a useful first-order indicator of anomalous behavior at the population level, but it is not calibrated to the specific behavioral envelope of any organizational role, and its accuracy in distinguishing normal high-tempo operational behavior from actual compromise depends on factors the platform has no visibility into.

## Chapter 9 — Conditional Access — Signal-Based Policy Without Structural Inheritance

### Architecture and Native Capabilities

Conditional Access is Entra ID's authentication policy engine. It evaluates every authentication request against a configured set of policies and determines whether to allow access, require additional verification, or block the request entirely. The evaluation model is comprehensive-by-default: all Conditional Access policies whose assignment conditions match the authentication event are evaluated simultaneously, and the grant controls are applied in a least-privilege manner — if any matching policy requires MFA, MFA is required regardless of whether other matching policies do not. Policy assignment conditions include the user and group identity of the authenticating principal, the application or service principal being accessed, the device platform and compliance state reported by Intune, the network location expressed as a named location or IP range, the sign-in risk level from Entra ID Protection, the user risk level, and the authentication strength of the method used to authenticate. Grant controls specify what the user must do or prove to satisfy the policy: completing MFA, presenting a compliant device, presenting a hybrid Azure AD joined device, using an approved client application, or satisfying a defined authentication strength. Session controls extend enforcement beyond the grant decision: sign-in frequency controls how long a session remains valid before re-authentication is required, Conditional Access App Control proxies application sessions through Defender for Cloud Apps for real-time monitoring, and application-enforced restrictions pass device compliance and domain join state into the application for session-level enforcement.

### Limitations of the Conditional Access Policy Model

Conditional Access policies apply to authentication events. A session that passes the Conditional Access evaluation at the moment of authentication is not re-evaluated by Conditional Access for the duration of that session unless the sign-in frequency control causes a re-authentication prompt. Changes to a user's group membership, device compliance state, or risk level that occur after session establishment do not retroactively affect the current session — they affect only future authentication events. This means that the enforcement window for Conditional Access is discrete rather than continuous: the policy state at authentication time determines session access, and the organization's security posture between authentication events is governed by controls outside the Conditional Access engine.

Conditional Access has no concept of organizational structure. Policies target users and groups explicitly. There is no mechanism by which a policy can target "all users in division X and all subordinate organizational units" unless a group exists that contains exactly those users, and that group must be maintained as the organizational structure evolves. In a large federal agency with complex organizational boundaries, the number of Conditional Access policies required to express differentiated access rules across organizational segments, application types, device tiers, network locations, and risk levels grows combinatorially. Each additional organizational segment that warrants differentiated treatment requires at minimum one additional policy. Each additional application type or network boundary multiplies the policy count further. The result in large, complex tenants is a policy set of dozens or hundreds of individual policies whose combined effect is difficult to reason about holistically, impossible to verify for completeness without custom tooling, and challenging to maintain as the organization and its applications evolve over time. The platform provides a "report-only" mode for evaluating the impact of a new policy before enforcement, which is a valuable capability, but it evaluates individual policies in isolation, not the combined effect of the entire policy set against a given user and device combination.

## Chapter 10 — Microsoft Intune — Device Management Without Organizational Inheritance

### Architecture and Native Capabilities

Microsoft Intune is Microsoft's cloud-native unified endpoint management platform, providing Mobile Device Management and Mobile Application Management capabilities for Windows, macOS, iOS, iPadOS, and Android devices. Device enrollment into Intune occurs through multiple channels: Windows Autopilot for zero-touch provisioning of new Windows devices; the Company Portal application for user-initiated enrollment on personal or corporate devices; co-management enrollment for devices already managed by Configuration Manager; and bulk enrollment through provisioning packages or device enrollment programs for iOS and Android. Once enrolled, a device receives its assigned configuration profiles, compliance policies, application assignments, and endpoint security policies from the Intune service, applying them through the management protocol appropriate for the device platform — OMA-DM for Windows, MDM for Apple platforms, Android Management API for Android Enterprise.

Windows configuration is delivered through three profile types. Settings Catalog profiles expose individual Windows configuration settings through a searchable interface that draws from the same Windows CSP registry as the underlying MDM protocol, organized by category. Administrative Template profiles in Intune expose the same ADMX-backed settings that Group Policy Administrative Templates provide, but delivered through the MDM channel rather than through the Group Policy Client service. Custom OMA-URI profiles allow administrators to configure any Windows CSP setting directly by specifying the full CSP path and the value to apply, providing a fallback for settings not yet exposed through the Settings Catalog or Administrative Template interfaces. Endpoint security policies deliver purpose-built profiles for Microsoft Defender Antivirus configuration, Windows Firewall rules, Endpoint Detection and Response onboarding, Attack Surface Reduction rules, and Account Protection settings. Application deployment through Intune supports Win32 applications delivered as .intunewin packages, Line-of-Business applications from MSIX or MSI packages, Microsoft Store applications, and web link shortcuts, with deployment intent settings of Required, Available, or Uninstall applied to user and device groups.

### Limitations of the Intune Policy Model

Intune's policy targeting model is entirely group-based. Every policy — every compliance policy, every configuration profile, every endpoint security policy, every application assignment — is deployed by assigning it to one or more Entra ID user or device groups. There is no inheritance model. A configuration profile assigned to a group representing the Washington Division does not automatically apply to members of groups representing units within the Washington Division. Each assignment relationship between a policy and a population must be created explicitly. If a new organizational unit is established and a new group is created to represent it, no existing policy automatically applies to the members of that new group because no policy has been explicitly assigned to it. The administrator must identify every policy that should apply to the new unit and create an explicit assignment for each one. There is no native mechanism for deriving which policies should apply to a new organizational segment from its position in an organizational hierarchy, because no organizational hierarchy exists in the targeting model.

Scope tags provide a mechanism for limiting which administrators can see and manage specific Intune resources — devices, applications, policies — by tagging resources and restricting administrator visibility to resources carrying their assigned tags. Scope tags address the delegation problem at the administrative visibility level: a regional Intune administrator tagged for the West region cannot see or modify devices and policies tagged for the East region. But scope tags do not create policy inheritance. Tagging a device with a regional scope tag does not cause any policy associated with that region to apply to the device. The scope tag controls administrative access to the resource, not the policy posture of the resource itself. The co-management model with Configuration Manager, used by organizations transitioning from the on-premises management model, introduces a split state in which some policy categories are governed by Intune — device compliance, modern application deployment, Windows Update for Business — while others remain governed by Configuration Manager — application deployment via SCCM, custom script execution, some endpoint security policies — and the workload split must be configured and maintained explicitly for every applicable policy category. There is no native model in Intune for the concept that devices belonging to one organizational segment should receive a superset of the policies applied to devices in a parent organizational segment. Each policy association must be constructed, verified, and maintained as an independent assignment.

## Chapter 11 — Azure Arc — Hybrid Management Without a Common Identity Model

### Architecture and Native Capabilities

Azure Arc extends the Azure control plane to servers that are not hosted in Azure. The Connected Machine Agent, installed on a Windows or Linux server running on-premises, in another cloud, or at an edge location, registers the server as a resource in the Azure Resource Manager. Once enrolled, the Arc-enabled server appears as an Azure resource of the type microsoft.hybridcompute/machines and can be governed through Azure Policy, monitored through Azure Monitor, and administered through Azure RBAC, exactly as if it were an Azure virtual machine. The Azure Monitor Agent can be deployed as an Arc extension to collect Windows event logs, performance counters, and custom log sources into a Log Analytics workspace, feeding the same analytics and alerting infrastructure that monitors Azure-native workloads. Microsoft Defender for Servers integration extends Endpoint Detection and Response coverage to Arc-enrolled servers through the Defender for Endpoint sensor, providing behavioral threat detection and attack surface reduction on on-premises and multi-cloud server workloads.

Azure Policy Guest Configuration extends the policy enforcement model to operating system settings on Arc-enrolled servers. Guest Configuration policies express desired state using PowerShell Desired State Configuration resources and evaluate or enforce those settings on the server through a local extension that runs the DSC assessment. The Azure Policy compliance dashboard reports the compliance state of Arc-enrolled servers against assigned Guest Configuration policies alongside the compliance state of Azure resources, providing a unified view within the scope of the Azure Resource Manager hierarchy. The Update Manager capability provides centralized visibility into patch assessment and update deployment for Arc-enrolled servers, allowing administrators to schedule patching windows and report on patch compliance without requiring the on-premises WSUS infrastructure that Configuration Manager traditionally provided. The Change Tracking and Inventory extension tracks changes to installed software, Windows services, Linux daemons, registry keys, and files on Arc-enrolled servers, generating change records in a Log Analytics workspace for audit and investigation purposes.

### Limitations: The Identity Fragmentation Problem

Azure Arc enrolls servers into the Azure Resource Manager, but the resource hierarchy into which they are enrolled — management groups, subscriptions, resource groups — is an Azure administrative and billing taxonomy, not an organizational governance taxonomy. The management group tree is constructed by Azure administrators to serve Azure governance purposes: RBAC delegation, Azure Policy scope, and subscription organization. It is maintained independently of every other organizational model in every other Microsoft tool. An Arc-enrolled server that was previously managed by a specific Organizational Unit in Active Directory carries no awareness of that OU relationship after Arc enrollment. The Arc agent does not read the machine's OU membership from AD. It does not import the GPO settings that were applied to the machine under its previous governance model. It does not replicate the on-premises organizational context into the Azure Resource Manager. The server is placed in a resource group by the administrator who performs the Arc enrollment, and the resource group's position in the management group hierarchy is the entirety of its governance context within Azure.

The identity fragmentation created by Arc enrollment is particularly significant for server governance. A single physical server in a federal environment can simultaneously hold three distinct identity representations in three separate systems: a computer object in Active Directory, carrying the machine's domain membership, OU placement, GPO application history, and Kerberos identity; a device object in Entra ID, created either through hybrid Azure AD join or through direct Entra ID registration, carrying the device's compliance state and Conditional Access signals; and a resource object in the Azure Resource Manager via Arc, carrying the machine's Azure RBAC assignments, Policy compliance state, and extension inventory. These three representations are not natively linked. There is no attribute on the Arc resource that references the AD computer object, no attribute on the Entra ID device object that references the Arc resource, and no native query that traverses all three systems to produce a unified view of a single physical machine's identity and governance context. Administrators who need to understand the complete governance posture of a server must query each system independently and correlate the results by machine name or IP address — correlation that is fragile, manual, and not guaranteed to be current.

## Chapter 12 — Azure Policy — Enforcement Without Cross-Platform Coherence

### Architecture and Native Capabilities

Azure Policy is the governance enforcement engine for the Azure Resource Manager ecosystem. Policy definitions express rules in JSON that evaluate the properties of Azure resources against specified conditions and apply effects when those conditions are met or violated. The built-in policy library covers thousands of configurations drawn from security benchmarks — the Microsoft Cloud Security Benchmark, CIS Controls, NIST SP 800-53, FedRAMP, and others — regulatory compliance frameworks, and operational standards. Policy effects range in their aggressiveness: Audit records non-compliant resources in the compliance dashboard without blocking them; Deny prevents resource creation or modification that would violate the policy; Append adds specified fields to the resource payload during creation; Modify changes resource properties during creation or update; DeployIfNotExists deploys a related resource or configuration if it does not exist on the target resource; and AuditIfNotExists audits the absence of a related resource or configuration. Policies are grouped into initiatives — collections of related policies — that can be assigned together as a unit, simplifying the management of complex compliance frameworks.

Policy assignment scope follows the Azure Resource Manager hierarchy: management groups, subscriptions, resource groups, and individual resources. An initiative assigned at the management group level applies to all subscriptions and resource groups within that management group, and remediation tasks can automatically bring non-compliant resources into compliance by triggering the deployIfNotExists effect against identified gaps. The compliance dashboard presents aggregate compliance percentages for each assigned policy and initiative, allowing organizations to track their posture against the assigned policy set and drill down to specific non-compliant resources. The Guest Configuration integration extends this enforcement surface to the operating system layer of Arc-enrolled servers, as described in the preceding chapter.

### Limitations of Azure Policy's Scope and Integration

Azure Policy operates exclusively on Azure Resource Manager resources. Its scope hierarchy is the Azure administrative taxonomy, not the organizational governance taxonomy. When a policy is assigned at the management group level, it applies to all Azure subscriptions and resource groups within that management group — a scope defined by the Azure billing and administrative hierarchy, which may or may not correspond to the organization's actual governance boundaries. The management group tree must be constructed and maintained independently of any other organizational model, and its structure reflects the Azure resource organization decisions made during Azure adoption, which are frequently driven by billing requirements and Azure RBAC delegation needs rather than by the organizational governance structure of the agency.

Compliance reporting in Azure Policy is scoped to the Azure resource hierarchy. The compliance dashboard reports on the compliance state of Azure resources and Arc-enrolled servers within the assigned scope. It cannot aggregate compliance reporting across a combined surface that includes Entra ID objects, Intune-managed devices, AD-joined machines, and Azure resources in a single view. An organization that wants a unified compliance posture across all four of those surfaces must construct that view through custom queries in Microsoft Sentinel, Azure Monitor, or the Defender portal — each of which requires custom data collection and custom query development to produce a result that is structurally coherent. The Guest Configuration capability for Arc-enrolled servers enforces operating system settings expressed as DSC configurations, but those DSC configurations must be authored independently of the on-premises GPO library. There is no native tool that converts GPO settings to Guest Configuration DSC resources. Each policy area must be re-expressed in the DSC language by engineers who understand both the GPO setting and its DSC equivalent. There is no native integration between Azure Policy compliance state and Conditional Access grant decisions, Intune device compliance status, or Entra ID Governance access review results.

## Chapter 13 — Active Directory DNS and Azure DNS — Two Resolution Models with No Native Bridge

### AD-Integrated DNS: Architecture and Capabilities

Active Directory-integrated DNS zones store zone data in the Active Directory domain partition or in dedicated application directory partitions, rather than in flat zone files on individual DNS server instances. This integration provides several significant operational advantages. Zone data replicates automatically with the AD replication topology, eliminating the separate zone transfer mechanism of traditional DNS and ensuring that every domain controller hosting the DNS Server role has a consistent, current copy of the zone. Dynamic DNS registration allows domain-joined clients to register and update their own A and PTR records automatically through the DNS client service, keeping the zone current as clients receive new IP addresses from DHCP. Scavenging removes stale records that were not refreshed within the configured aging window, preventing the accumulation of records that no longer correspond to active clients. SRV records published by the Netlogon service on domain controllers — records of the form \_kerberos.\_tcp.dc.\_msdcs.domain.com, \_ldap.\_tcp.dc.\_msdcs.domain.com, and others — provide the service locator infrastructure that allows Kerberos clients, LDAP clients, and replication partners to discover domain controllers without static configuration. Conditional forwarders allow the AD DNS infrastructure to resolve names in external domains by forwarding queries to designated servers, supporting the split-brain DNS designs and inter-domain name resolution patterns common in large federal environments.

### Azure DNS: Architecture and Capabilities

Azure DNS provides two distinct resolution services: Public Zones for authoritative DNS for internet-facing names, and Private Zones for name resolution within Azure virtual networks. Azure DNS Public Zones are globally redundant authoritative name servers hosted by Microsoft, serving DNS responses for delegated public domain names with high availability and global anycast distribution. Azure DNS Private Zones provide automatic name registration for Azure virtual machines connected to linked virtual networks and are the primary mechanism for resolving private endpoint names for Azure PaaS services. The Azure DNS Private Resolver provides inbound and outbound DNS resolution endpoints that allow Azure virtual networks to receive DNS queries from on-premises networks via hybrid connectivity, and to forward DNS queries from Azure to on-premises DNS servers through Forwarding Rulesets. This Private Resolver capability is the primary technical mechanism for bridging the AD-integrated DNS model and the Azure DNS model during hybrid connectivity scenarios.

### Limitations: The Decomposition of the DNS-AD Fabric

The AD-integrated DNS model is functionally inseparable from the domain controller infrastructure. Every domain controller that hosts the DNS Server role is simultaneously a replication partner for zone data and an authenticator for dynamic DNS registrations. As domain controllers are decommissioned in the process of migrating away from Active Directory, the DNS infrastructure that every domain-joined machine depends on for service location disappears with them. This is not a gradual degradation — it is a cliff. When the last domain controller in a domain is decommissioned, the SRV records that every Kerberos client queries to find a KDC become unresolvable, and every authentication attempt against the old domain fails. The complete decommissioning of AD requires that every consumer of AD DNS — every application, every service, every client device that resolves domain-relative names — be migrated to an alternative resolution model before the AD DNS infrastructure is removed. There is no native Microsoft tool that comprehensively identifies every DNS consumer before decommissioning begins.

Azure DNS Private Zones do not replicate or import from AD-integrated DNS zones. The records that exist in the AD zone must be manually recreated or imported into Azure DNS through a scripted migration process that is not natively provided by either platform. The SRV records that AD publishes for its service locator model have no equivalent in a cloud-only identity architecture: there are no Kerberos KDCs to locate, no global catalog servers to locate, and no LDAP DCs to locate once the domain is decommissioned, and therefore no SRV records to publish. Applications that were built with hard-coded dependencies on AD service locator SRV records will fail to resolve those records and must be reconfigured before the AD DNS infrastructure is removed. The Private Resolver bridging model requires Azure hybrid connectivity — an ExpressRoute circuit or a Site-to-Site VPN — and correct configuration of Forwarding Rulesets to ensure that on-premises clients can resolve Azure private endpoint names during the transition period. The DNS dependency mapping required to plan this transition safely is a pre-migration requirement that the platform does not assist with natively.

## Chapter 14 — Active Directory Certificate Services and Cloud PKI — Certificate Governance at the Point of Issuance

### AD CS: Architecture and Native Capabilities

Active Directory Certificate Services implements a Public Key Infrastructure integrated with the Active Directory directory service, providing a CA hierarchy that issues certificates to domain-joined machines, users, and services through an enrollment process that uses the directory for identity validation. The CA hierarchy typically consists of an offline root CA and one or more online subordinate issuing CAs. Certificate templates, stored in the Configuration partition of the Active Directory forest and replicated to every domain controller, define every property of certificates issued by the enterprise CA: the key algorithm and size, the key usage and extended key usage values, the subject name source (whether derived from the directory or supplied by the requester), the validity period, the enrollment permissions that control which security principals may request each template type, and the application policies. Auto-enrollment is the mechanism by which domain-joined machines and users receive certificates automatically: Group Policy delivers the auto-enrollment configuration to domain-joined clients, which then query the CA for all templates they are eligible to receive and request certificates accordingly, with no user interaction required. This auto-enrollment model is the basis for certificate-based authentication in the AD environment, for LDAPS on domain controllers, for domain controller authentication certificates, for Wi-Fi EAP-TLS authentication, and for code signing in managed environments.

The ESC vulnerability classes — ESC1 through ESC8, as documented by SpecterOps researchers — represent the most consequential certificate template misconfiguration patterns in the AD CS ecosystem. ESC1 allows low-privileged users to request certificates with arbitrary Subject Alternative Names, enabling impersonation of any identity including domain administrators. ESC2 allows low-privileged users to request certificates with the Any Purpose EKU. ESC3 allows enrollment agent abuse to request certificates on behalf of other users. ESC4 allows modification of certificate template settings by unprivileged principals. ESC5, ESC6, ESC7, and ESC8 exploit various combinations of CA object permissions, EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2 flag settings, CA officer privilege abuse, and NTLM relay against the AD CS web enrollment interface. These vulnerabilities are endemic in enterprise deployments because certificate template permissions accumulate over time without audit, and the AD CS management tooling does not provide a native risk assessment of the template library.

### Microsoft Cloud PKI: Architecture and Capabilities
