Microsoft Cloud PKI is a managed CA service administered through the Intune admin center, introduced as a component of the Microsoft Intune Suite. It provides a cloud-hosted CA hierarchy — a Microsoft-managed root CA with a customer-managed issuing CA beneath it, or a BYOCA model in which an existing on-premises issuing CA is imported — and delivers certificates to Intune-managed devices through SCEP and PKCS Intune certificate profiles. The SCEP profile type uses the Simple Certificate Enrollment Protocol to allow devices to request certificates directly from a SCEP endpoint associated with the Cloud PKI issuing CA, with device identity validated through the Intune service. The PKCS profile type delivers certificates generated by the CA through the Intune service to the device. Entra ID Certificate-Based Authentication extends the certificate model to Entra ID authentication, allowing users to authenticate with smart card-equivalent certificate credentials, satisfying phishing-resistant MFA requirements without requiring hardware tokens.

### Limitations of the PKI Transition

The AD CS model creates long-lived dependencies that extend far beyond the CA infrastructure itself. Every certificate issued by an enterprise CA contains, embedded in its AIA and CRL Distribution Point extensions, the URLs at which the issuing CA's OCSP responder and Certificate Revocation List can be found. These URLs must remain reachable for the complete validity period of every certificate issued — which may be one, two, three, or more years — because any relying party that validates a certificate chain will attempt to retrieve the CRL or OCSP response from those embedded URLs to confirm the certificate has not been revoked. Decommissioning the on-premises CA infrastructure before all issued certificates have expired will cause certificate validation failures for every relying party that attempts to verify a chain anchored to the old CA's root. The scope of this problem is not knowable without a comprehensive inventory of every issued certificate, every relying party, and every validity period — and that inventory does not exist natively in any Microsoft tool.

Microsoft Cloud PKI is a capable managed service that addresses the operational burden of maintaining CA infrastructure, but it does not replicate the full depth of AD CS template customization for specialized use cases. The certificate profile model in Intune covers the majority of common certificate types, but organizations with highly specialized certificate configurations — custom EKUs, complex subject name constructions, unusual validity periods tied to regulatory requirements — may find that the available configuration surface does not accommodate their existing template definitions without modification. The SCEP and PKCS delivery mechanisms are available only to Intune-enrolled devices. Servers that are not enrolled in Intune, non-domain-joined systems outside the Intune management boundary, and legacy applications that request certificates directly from the CA through the DCOM or CEP/CES protocols cannot receive certificates from Cloud PKI without additional integration infrastructure. The transition from AD CS to Cloud PKI therefore requires both a CA migration — establishing trust in the new CA's certificates throughout the environment — and a certificate delivery migration — ensuring that every certificate consumer has a path to receive certificates from the new delivery mechanism. There is no native Microsoft tool that maps every certificate consumer before the migration begins.

## Chapter 15 — The Defender Suite — Security Intelligence Without Governance Coherence

### Defender for Identity

Microsoft Defender for Identity is a threat detection product focused exclusively on the Active Directory environment. It operates by installing a sensor on each domain controller in the domain — or on a dedicated server receiving a mirrored copy of the DC's network traffic — and reading the authentication and directory events generated by the domain controller: Kerberos service ticket requests and responses, NTLM authentication events, LDAP queries, directory replication events, and Windows event log records from the DC's security and system logs. From this telemetry, Defender for Identity builds entity profiles for users, computers, and groups, recording their authentication patterns, LDAP query activity, lateral movement indicators, and directory access behavior over time. It detects specific attack techniques against these baselines: Kerberoasting is identified by abnormal NTLM or Kerberos activity patterns; DCSync attacks are detected by identifying non-DC accounts performing directory replication; Pass-the-Hash and Pass-the-Ticket attacks are identified through anomalous credential usage patterns; LDAP reconnaissance is identified by unusual enumeration queries. The entity timeline for any user or device provides a chronological record of the entity's authentication and directory activity that can be used for incident investigation.

### Defender for Endpoint, Defender for Cloud Apps, and Defender for Cloud

Defender for Endpoint provides behavioral threat detection and response on enrolled endpoints — Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, and Android — through a sensor that collects process, network, file, and registry telemetry and evaluates it against detection rules and machine learning models. It surfaces attack timeline reconstructions that show the chain of events from initial access through lateral movement and objective execution, device and software inventory with vulnerability exposure scoring, and integration with Conditional Access through the device compliance signal it contributes to Intune. Defender for Cloud Apps provides visibility into cloud service usage, generating shadow IT discovery reports from proxy or firewall log ingestion, governing OAuth application permissions registered in Entra ID through app governance policies, and applying session-level monitoring and control to supported applications through Conditional Access App Control. Defender for Cloud provides security posture management for Azure resources and Arc-enrolled servers, generating a Secure Score that reflects the proportion of security recommendations that have been implemented, and surfacing attack path analysis that identifies lateral movement paths through the Azure resource inventory toward high-value targets.

### The Coherence Gap Across the Defender Suite

The Defender products individually provide security signal of genuine value within their respective domains. The limitation is not in the individual products. It is in the fact that the domains do not share a common organizational model, and the signals they generate are therefore not naturally correlatable in organizational terms. An alert in Defender for Identity indicating that a specific user account is performing suspicious LDAP reconnaissance against the domain is expressed in terms of the Entra ID object identifier of that user and the samAccountName of the account. A corresponding Conditional Access sign-in risk elevation for that user is expressed in terms of the user's principal name and the sign-in event's correlation ID. A Defender for Endpoint alert on that user's workstation is expressed in terms of the device's Entra ID device ID and the machine's DNS name. A Defender for Cloud Apps alert about anomalous download activity in that user's SharePoint sessions is expressed in terms of the user's UPN and the application session identifier. Each of these signals refers to the same individual but through different identifier systems, and none of them is expressed in terms of the user's organizational placement — their division, their role, their access tier, their peer group — because no system in the stack maintains that organizational model as structured, queryable data.

Correlating these signals requires custom query work in Microsoft Sentinel or the unified Defender XDR portal, where analysts can write KQL queries that join tables across the different product telemetry streams using shared identifier fields. This correlation is technically possible and is the intended investigative workflow in the Microsoft security architecture. But the result of the correlation is a timeline of events for a specific individual expressed in terms of object identifiers and event timestamps. It does not produce an answer to the question that organizational security management requires: what is the scope and pattern of this activity relative to the organizational structure? Are other users in the same division exhibiting similar patterns? Does the activity pattern suggest a targeted operation against a specific organizational unit? Is the observed behavior consistent with the role and access profile of the user's organizational position? These questions require the analyst to know the organizational context from sources outside the Defender suite — an organizational chart, an HR system, an access inventory maintained separately — and to apply that context manually to the raw telemetry. The platform does not provide it.

## Chapter 16 — The Aggregate Picture

### What Has Been Described

The fifteen chapters preceding this one have described, in sequence, the components of the Microsoft identity and governance stack as they actually function: what each tool natively provides, where its native capabilities end, and what questions it cannot answer without custom engineering. The picture that emerges from this accounting is not of an inadequate platform. Entra ID is a capable, globally available, protocol-compliant identity service. Intune is a mature unified endpoint management platform with a deep Windows configuration surface. Azure Arc extends the Azure control plane to on-premises and multi-cloud workloads in a technically sound manner. Azure Policy provides enforceable, auditable governance rules for Azure resources. Microsoft Cloud PKI removes significant operational burden from certificate infrastructure management. The Defender suite generates security intelligence of genuine quality within each of its operational domains. These are not weak tools. They are individually strong. The problem that this document has traced is not located in any individual tool.

### The Structural Absence

The problem is located in the absence of the substrate that connects them. Active Directory was imperfect in important ways. Its perimeter-based security model was designed for a threat landscape that no longer exists. Its privileged access model creates catastrophic blast radius at the Domain Admin tier. Its GPO model is opaque and difficult to audit programmatically. Its attack surface is extensively mapped by adversaries who have invested years in techniques that exploit structural properties of Kerberos and NTLM. The case for migrating away from Active Directory as the primary identity and governance substrate is clear and well-founded. But Active Directory was coherent. Every object existed in the same directory. Every governance mechanism shared the same structural model. The OU, the GPO, the DNS record, the certificate template, the computer object, and the user object all related to one another through a single inheritance framework. An administrator who understood the OU tree understood the policy model, the delegation model, the DNS model, and the certificate enrollment model simultaneously, because they were all expressions of the same structure. A governance question about any object in the directory could be answered by traversing the directory through LDAP. The organizational context of any identity was a property of the directory itself, not a custom attribute that had to be maintained through separate integration work.

When that infrastructure is replaced with Entra ID, Intune, Azure Arc, Azure DNS, Microsoft Cloud PKI, and the Defender suite, the individual capabilities arrive but the substrate does not. Each tool brings a different answer to the most fundamental governance question: what is the organizational structure that defines which policies apply, which administrators have authority, and which resources are in scope? Entra ID answers with the flat tenant and the Administrative Unit. Intune answers with explicitly assigned groups. Azure Arc answers with the Azure resource group and management group. Azure Policy answers with the management group hierarchy. Azure DNS answers with the virtual network link. Microsoft Cloud PKI answers with the Intune certificate profile and device group. The Defender suite answers with object identifiers in separate telemetry streams. These answers are not wrong — they are each internally coherent within their own tool's model. But they are different answers, expressed in different data models, with no native mechanism that translates between them or aggregates them into a single structural view of the organization.

### The Honest Accounting

Federal agencies completing the migration from Active Directory to the Microsoft cloud stack frequently discover this gap not at the planning stage but at the point of decommissioning. The migration proceeds tool by tool: identities are synchronized, devices are enrolled, servers are Arc-connected, DNS is partially bridged, Cloud PKI is stood up, Defender products are deployed. Each step is technically successful. Each tool is operating within its design parameters. At the moment the decision is made to decommission the last domain controller, the full scope of what is being removed becomes visible: not just an authentication service, not just a DNS zone, not just a policy engine, but the single structural model that gave each of those functions its organizational meaning. What remains after decommissioning is a collection of individually functional tools that collectively cannot express, without custom engineering, what the on-premises infrastructure expressed as a matter of course: where an identity sits in the organization, what policies flow from that position, who has delegated authority over that segment, and what the blast radius of any change to that segment's governance would be.

This is the honest accounting. The tools are ready. The substrate that connects them is not natively present. Organizations completing this migration must either accept a governance posture that is structurally less coherent than what they are replacing — accepting that policy application will be explicit rather than inherited, that delegation will be flat rather than hierarchical, that organizational context will be a custom attribute rather than a structural property of the directory — or they must construct the missing substrate themselves, building the organizational model, the inheritance logic, the delegation framework, and the cross-tool correlation that the platform does not natively provide. That construction is achievable. It is not trivial. And it is not optional for organizations whose governance obligations require continuous, verifiable, structurally coherent management of identity and policy across the full surface of their IT environment.

This document was prepared as a technical analysis for senior federal IT architects and governance engineers. All platform capabilities and limitations described reflect the native design of the Microsoft identity and governance stack as of the document preparation date. May 2026.
